data.description:
5-Year Morningstar Rating™

Class A Institutional Class

The Morningstar Rating is for the indicated share classes only as of 03/31/26; other classes may have different performance characteristics. The Morningstar ratings for the overall, three-, five- and ten-year periods for Class A shares are 3 stars, 3 stars, 4 stars and 3 stars and for Institutional Class shares are 3 stars, 3 stars, 4 stars and 3 stars among 98, 98, 92 and 66 Commodities Broad Basket funds respectively, and are based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure.

The current environment remains favorable for selective overweight allocations to alternative asset classes, such as commodities, offering diversification benefits and incremental return potential.

Fund strategy

  • Offers direct exposure to a select mixed basket of commodities
  • Uses an actively managed, research-driven investment approach to identify the best ideas from across the global commodity spectrum
  • Managed by a seasoned investment team that averages over 25 years of experience in pure commodities markets

Expense ratio

Inst. 0.86% 0.86%
A 1.11% 1.11%

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 09/30/2026, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Commodity Strategy Fund returned 23.89% for the quarter ending March 31, 2026.
  • The fund's benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return, returned 24.41% for the same period.
  • Monthly fund performance is available online at Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US .

Market overview

For the quarter ending March 31, 2026, commodities gained 24.41%, as measured by the Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return, the fund's benchmark.

Within commodities, the best-performing sector was energy, which returned 58.59%, driven by broad-based strength across refined products. Heating oil and gas oil led gains, surging more than 100% over the previous quarter, as escalating Middle East geopolitical tensions raised concerns about supply disruptions and tightened global distillate balances. Natural gas detracted modestly due to warmer-than-expected winter weather and ample U.S. supply. Strength in crude oil and refined products more than offset this weakness.

Precious metals posted positive but volatile performance, returning 7.59%, with strong gains earlier in the quarter partially reversed in March. Gold and silver benefited initially from safe-haven demand amid geopolitical uncertainty and monetary policy concerns, before weakening late in the quarter, as a stronger U.S. dollar and higher U.S. Treasury yields weighed on investor demand.

Industrial metals delivered modest gains, reflecting mixed underlying dynamics. Aluminum outperformed on supply disruptions, low inventories and rising energy costs, while copper declined modestly as macro headwinds and surplus concerns offset earlier support from structural demand tied to electrification and Artificial Intelligence (AI)-related investment.

Agriculture finished the quarter higher, returning 7.07%, led by strong performance in soy oil and wheat. Soy oil surged on improved renewable diesel margins and tighter U.S. biofuel mandates, while Kansas wheat and Chicago wheat benefited from worsening U.S. drought conditions that heightened yield concerns and prompted short covering. Other agricultural commodities were more mixed, resulting in moderate overall gains.

Softs were the primary drag, declining 8.08% over the quarter. Steep losses in cocoa and coffee outweighed late-quarter rebounds in sugar and cotton. Cocoa prices remained under pressure for most of the quarter as earlier supply shortages reversed into surplus conditions, though March saw a partial recovery driven by renewed concerns about weather and disease in West Africa.

Livestock posted modest gains, with strength in live cattle and feeder cattle, which was supported by tight slaughter levels, improving beef demand and firm boxed beef prices. Lean hogs lagged for the quarter, as high slaughter volumes and softer pork cutout values pressured prices after early-year strength.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Columbia Commodity Fund's Institutional Class shares returned 23.89% for the quarter ending March 31, 2026. The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return, which returned 24.41% over the same period.

The fund's largest detractor from relative performance was its curve positioning (security selection) within energy. Energy exposure to longer-dated contacts in brent crude oil and heating oil detracted from relative performance. The level of crude oil and refined product supply disruption, stemming from the military action in Iran, was most acutely priced in the front contracts (where the portfolio is typically underweight). The market is currently pricing a relatively short duration of disruption and thus deferred positions have been slower to respond compared with prompt pricing. The longer the energy-related disruption persists, the more deferred parts of curve will begin to re-price higher. Curve positioning within grains and industrial metals also weighed moderately on performance.

The fund's active allocation decisions contributed to relative performance but was not enough to offset the negative effects from curve positioning. An overweight to energy was the largest contributor to relative performance. Within energy, overweight exposure to unleaded gas and heating oil were the main contributors to performance. An overweight to livestock, specifically feeder cattle, also aided performance over the quarter.

Outlook

The current environment remains favorable for selective overweight allocations to alternative asset classes, such as commodities, offering diversification benefits and incremental return potential. With inflation concerns still prominent among investors, commodities continue to represent a strategically compelling thematic overweight within portfolios. Similar to 2022, commodities have been able to deliver positive returns to provide diversification benefits to equity and fixed-income asset classes driven by an inflationary shock. Longer term, we may be entering a world where hard resources will play an increasingly important role in national security interests. This will underpin the commodity asset class as an important strategic allocation in investor's portfolios.

Our modeling framework, underpinned by solid fundamental signs, continues to support a constructive outlook for commodities in the months ahead. We will continue to monitor the impact from the extreme crude oil supply disruption and rely on our systematic, research-driven investment process, which is grounded in academically validated sources of return and informed by cross-asset signals, to help manage portfolio risk.

data.title: Blanket Sell-Off In Software Is Creating Wild Mispricing
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the NZS Capital Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter.

Software – Are you as sick of the “baby with the bathwater” idiom as we are?

It was recently suggested to us, and Gemini confirmed (of course), that it is highly unlikely a baby has ever been accidentally thrown out with the bathwater. We formally propose a more realistic phrase: “pulling out flowers with the weeds” and welcome any other suggestions. Regardless of our linguistic quibble, we do believe it is most likely that the blanket sell-off across software due to the AI disruption narrative is creating some wild mispricing of certain software companies that we believe will ultimately be able to adapt to AI. At the highest level, we break the risks into two buckets: 1) AI displacement of the incumbents via AI-native startups or internal vibe coding, and 2) seat risk, or the risk of layoffs due to AI efficiencies negatively impacting the revenues of SaaS, which is often sold by the seat.

For the risk of displacement due to AI, we ultimately focus on the NZS, or non-zero sumness, of the software businesses in which we are investing. This is simply because companies that are creating more value than they take are going to be much harder to disrupt. In some cases, AI has turned once high-NZS software products into zero-sum propositions; e.g., if their value lies largely in the code, then that value has been commoditized and the NZS impaired. We sold monday.com (MNDY) last November because we believe that much of the value of a project management tool lies in the code. Sources of value that go beyond the code include systems of record (SOR) for business-critical information, trust, scaled network effects, niche workflow context, tight hardware-and-software integration, institutional familiarity, incumbency, etc. We compare that value to the price of the software, which generally represents ~0-2% of customer revenues for the software companies we invest in (low tariff extractors are a theme throughout our portfolio). Tyler Technologies (TYL), which we added in the quarter, provides software for government administrative purposes, including public administration, property appraisal, tax assessment, court management, and public safety. We'd argue that Tyler's software acts as mission critical SoR whose value is much more than just the code; yet, their product usually represents only a fraction of a percent of a customer's operating budget. Even setting aside the risk-averse nature of municipal government customers, such high levels of NZS seem quite unlikely to be internally vibe coded or ripped and replaced for an "AI-native" startup with no track record. Despite this positive outlook, Tyler's stock has fallen >40% in the past year. Ultimately, we think the most likely outcome is that AI agents will interact with the existing high-NZS SoR rather than replace them. That brings us to the second, and in our opinion much bigger, risk to software from AI. We think AI is much more focused on generating ROI through the elimination of labor than displacing existing software platforms. Given that we believe the risk to labor from AI is a credible (if not likely) concern, we've positioned our software exposure accordingly. Roughly 70% of our software holdings are primarily vertical market software, i.e., companies servicing a specific vertical rather than broad enterprises; we believe the seat risk is significantly lower in vertical market software businesses. These include semiconductor design (Cadence (CDNS) and Synopsys (SNPS)), government and law enforcement (Tyler and Axon (AXON)), and restaurants (Toast (TOST)). We also own Roper and Constellation Software, which both provide vertical market software to a variety of niche, disparate industries through a decentralized operating model. We've manually categorized each of the underlying businesses that make up Roper (ROP) (of which there are ~30) and Constellation Software (CNSWF) (of which there are ~1,000) and feel comfortable that the seat risk faced by their underlying businesses is minimal.

The next largest bucket of software that we own has a consumption or take-rate based pricing model. For example, JFrog's (FROG) binary artifactory is priced on the total amount of data storage and data transfer on its platform, which has seen dramatic growth due to the accelerated pace and volume of software being written by its customers enabled by vibe coding efficiencies. Snowflake (SNOW) and Cloudflare (NET) are optionality positions that also fit into this basket.

Higher seat-risk businesses are the smallest bucket of our software exposure and represent <3% of the total portfolio (Workday (WDAY), Atlassian (TEAM), and Figma (FIG)). Should these companies successfully adapt and layer on consumption-based revenue streams to offset potential seat degradation (or should the seat degradation thesis simply not play out), they will likely be among the most asymmetric benefactors.

Lastly, in addition to focusing on companies creating high NZS and generally having less seat risk, we have also attempted to further push out the middle in software to companies that are truly “GAAP cheap” (i.e., backing stock comp out of free cash flow) or highly asymmetric. As always, we hold our views on software with a loose grip, but at present we are excited about the opportunities to position our sector holdings afforded by the indiscriminate market sell-off.

DIFFERENCE (% NET) -5.61% -4.29% 3.27% 12.30% -15.40% 3.29% 46.66%

*Since inception: January 1, 2020; returns as of March 31, 2026.

Performance Overview

Equity markets declined in the first quarter of 2026, with particular weakness in growth stocks as the market grappled with increased uncertainty and upward bias to inflation and interest rate expectations. The portfolio underperformed the index due to this slump in growth equities and our software overweight. The resilient head of the portfolio did provide ballast, declining less than the index, while the optional tail saw a sharp correction given the growthier nature of the stocks that reside there.

In the quarter, only the Materials sector contributed positively to the portfolio’s absolute returns, though the semiconductor sub-sector also contributed strongly; Lam Research (LRCX), ASML Holding (ASML), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) were each top-five contributors to absolute returns. TSMC, ASML, and Lam Research continue to benefit from strong demand for compute in AI infrastructure, with ASML and Lam also benefitting from unprecedented demand for memory, which should fuel continued growth in semiconductor capital equipment spending. ARM Holdings (ARM) was also a top contributor following introduction of the company’s first in-house CPU design, for which it set impressive revenue growth targets based on customer commitments. Linde PLC (LIN), a global provider of industrial gases, rounded out the top-five contributors.

The largest sectors detracting from absolute returns were Information Technology, Financials, and Healthcare. Workday was the largest absolute detractor in the quarter, driven by increasing concerns over the potential disruption to SaaS platforms. Constellation Software and Atlassian, as well as other software companies in the portfolio, were similarly pressured by this growing narrative, as the market generally sold software indiscriminately. Gartner was weak on AI disruption concerns that were worsened by a disappointing outlook for 2026, which was generally in line with the market's myopic view of software. Danaher (DHR) was also a top-five detractor in the quarter.

Portfolio Positioning

Market weakness provided compelling new opportunities, heightening portfolio activity in the quarter. The resilient head of the portfolio outperformed the index in Q1, while the optional tail experienced a steeper decline. Following the outsized market sell-off, we believe valuations in certain pockets of growth equities are at the most attractive levels we have seen in some time. As such, we began harvesting from resilient positions and incrementally adding to optional ideas that have become increasingly compelling.

In addition to general weakness in growth equities, software fell sharply over AI disruption concerns. We continue to evolve the makeup of our software holdings and began adding to the sector in late February, as we think the broad-based software sell-off has likely provided some of the most compelling multi-year opportunities in the market. (There is an expanded discussion of our software positioning below.)

Healthcare has increased in weight in the portfolio in recent quarters as fundamentals have normalized after a long post-COVID hangover, and valuations have become less demanding. Increasing lifespans from GLP-1 adoption may provide tailwinds for several segments of the healthcare sector. Stryker, which had been on our watchlist as a strong fit for our framework, was added as a new resilient position after the valuation became more attractive. Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) was recently promoted from optional to resilient, and we introduced WuXi XDC as a new optionality position. These additions were partially funded by trims to Penumbra, which is being acquired by Boston Scientific (BSX). Other new additions to the portfolio outside of healthcare include optionality positions in Tyler Technologies (TYL) and Sabre Corp (SABR). Amidst elevated optionality turnover in the portfolio, we exited Procore (PCOR), Gartner (IT), Floor & Decor (FND), ServiceTitan (TTAN), ServiceNow (NOW), Cognex (CGNX), and Lemonade (LMND).

data.title: Microsoft: Preparing For Pivotal 'Show-Me' Q3 Earnings
data.description:
This upcoming Wednesday, nearly $12 trillion of market cap is going to report earnings, as Microsoft (MSFT) and its closest peers in Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META), take center stage.

In the past, all of them reporting on the same day would probably be viewed as a nice trivia fact, but nothing material. However, in the AI era, these companies have learned they don't want to disclose their hands too early.

There's a very good reason, in my mind, why they all aligned to report together, and as Microsoft comes to this print from a relatively weak position, down 20% from its highs, I call this a 'Show-Me' quarter for Microsoft, reiterating a 'Hold' rating.

Microsoft's AI Challenges

Many investors view almost any type of selloff in a high-quality name like Microsoft as a clear 'Green Flag' to start buying aggressively. I recently wrote an article explaining why Microsoft's 20% drawdown isn't as clear a no-brainer as some investors make it to be.

In short, I outlined Microsoft's inferior position relative to Google, Amazon, or Anthropic (ANTHRO), resulting from not having a competitive in-house chip in the market, not having an in-house model, and struggling to balance the compute needs of Azure and the rest of Microsoft.

In more detail, not having an in-house chip means that X amount of capex gives Microsoft less capacity relative to Amazon or Alphabet; Not having an in-house model means that OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic, and Google are better positioned to build AI-based products; and the compute allocation issue is a "brand" damage to Azure, as customers learned they are not prioritized.

This upcoming quarter is Microsoft's chance to prove me wrong. Let's preview each of the most important issues, one by one.

The Number One Business Line To Monitor Isn't Azure, It's M365

In recent years, investors have gotten used to focusing on the Azure number every quarter. Pretty much, if Azure beat expectations and Azure guidance was anything above consensus, the stock would go up, and vice versa.

This time around, my view is that Azure takes a back seat. Although I have some concerns about it, which we'll discuss in the next section, the truth is that demand is so high for cloud that it's not that much of a worry.

In contrast, there are significant questions about M365 right now. The biggest issue here is AI adoption, where Microsoft said they have 'only' 15 million Copilot seats, reflecting around 3% penetration among its users. For comparison, Gemini Enterprise AI has over 8 million users, a much higher penetration rate.

Commercial Seats growth has decelerated meaningfully in recent quarters, settling at 6%. M365 Commercial revenue is also decelerating, dropping to a low of 14% last quarter.

While some might say 14% isn't all that bad, the problem here is competition. As Anthropic grows exponentially in the enterprise, and OpenAI is also delivering strong growth, Microsoft is emerging as a laggard and a market share loser with its decelerating growth.

Management's guidance for the quarter reflects continued deceleration, and if they don't beat it, I believe investors will be highly disappointed. The only relief could come from Azure.

Azure Remains Crucial, Though

In my mind, the biggest risk to my skeptical view of Microsoft is a strategic shift, under which Microsoft management decides they should allocate more capacity to Azure to alleviate investors' criticism.

From the Q2 earnings call, CFO Amy Hood, regarding compute allocation:

The first thing we're doing is solving for the increased usage in sales and the accelerating pace of M365 Copilot as well as GitHub Copilot, our first-party apps. Then we make sure we're investing in the long-term nature of R&D and product innovation. And much of the acceleration that I think you've seen from us and products over the past a bit is coming because we are allocating GPUs and capacity to many of the talented AI people we've been hiring over the past years.

Then, when you end up, is that, you end up with the remainder going towards serving the Azure capacity that continues to grow in terms of demand. And a way to think about it, because I think, I get asked this question sometimes, is if I had taken the GPUs that just came online in Q1 and Q2 in terms of GPUs and allocated them all to Azure, the KPI would have been over 40.

The biggest issue with this quote is that M365 decelerated alongside Azure last quarter. If M365 is getting capacity priority, investors want to see it yielding results, not just being used as an excuse. For now, the only explanation is that this allocation isn't going towards M365 inference, but rather R&D.

So, one thing that management can do is simply decide to allocate more capacity to Azure, which should yield better-than-expected growth, and I don't expect the market to nitpick the reasons for it.

Last quarter, Azure was the only cloud that didn't accelerate, and backlog growth, normalized for OpenAI, was also the lowest among the three hyperscalers.

Simply put, if Azure comes last once again and does not accelerate while GCP and AWS do, this will be bad news for the bulls.

Then, It's About Capex, And Why Everybody Reports The Same Day

My next argument might sound somewhat stupid to some, but I firmly believe it has a good chance of being true.

For the December quarter, Microsoft and Meta reported earnings a week before Alphabet and Amazon. They have both guided for capital spending ahead of market expectations, but not even close to the same extent as Alphabet and Amazon did.

Indeed, the gap in spending between Google and Amazon vs. Microsoft is set to be the biggest in recent memory, with Alphabet surpassing Microsoft for the first time in I don't know how many years (possibly for the first time ever).

In case you forgot, market sentiment regarding hyperscaler capex in the early part of the year was very negative. Now, I think it's safe to say it's the other way around.

Therefore, I believe Microsoft doesn't want to show its hand when it comes to capex too early, and that it will escalate to meet the levels of Amazon and Alphabet.

Considering that Microsoft is yet to deploy Maia chips at a meaningful scale, effectively all of its capital spending on accelerators goes through Nvidia (NVDA), which takes a nice 75% cut on each sale. This means that each dollar of capacity spent for Microsoft yields less capacity relative to Amazon or Alphabet.

Moreover, while Alphabet has a whole portfolio of heavy internal needs for capacity across Gemini, Search, and YouTube, I think it's safe to say Amazon doesn't have that much more internal demand vs. Microsoft.

The fact that Amazon is spending so much, primarily for external AWS customers, stands in stark contrast to the quote we cited in the previous section.

Bottom line, I don't think investors are happy about Microsoft's relative conservatism this time around. So, their capex outlook is going to be critical in order to understand where they're heading.

Valuation

At $425 per share, Microsoft trades at 25 times forward earnings, and 22.5 times next year's estimates. This places Microsoft about 20% below its historical average, and below Google and Amazon, also counter to history.

That said, both Alphabet and Amazon are delivering accelerating growth at a larger scale, and neither of them has a huge software business that's facing the looming AI monster.

For context, other leading software companies like SAP (SAP), Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE), Workday (WDAY), ServiceNow (NOW), or Intuit (INTU) are trading more than 50% below their historical levels, far worse than Microsoft.

So, for Microsoft to return to the multiple from its glory days, it will need to prove that it belongs to the Amazon & Google group, not the lagging software names.

Conclusion

Microsoft is set to report earnings this Wednesday, April 29th, in what I define as a 'Show Me' quarter.

With so much talk about the market's irrational sell-off, it's time for Microsoft to deliver the goods. This means accelerating M365 growth, further Copilot adoption, and Azure acceleration.

Anything less than that will disappoint investors, and rightfully so.

In my view, there's no evidence that Microsoft has been able to get back on track in AI, and I don't view its management as the type that takes short-term decisions like allocating more capacity to Azure just to make investors happy.

As such, I remain skeptical about the stock here and reiterate a 'Hold' ahead of earnings.

data.title: Barron's Top 10 ESG Dogs Tag 4 As 'Safer' For April Dividends
data.description:
Foreword

This article is based on 10 top sustainable companies as determined by Calvert Research and Management's annual review of more than 230 Environmental, Social and Governance [ESG] performance indicators, such as workplace diversity, data security, and greenhouse-gas emissions, as reported in the April 17, 2026, edition of Barron's weekly.

Barron's 10 Most Sustainable Companies for 2026

Companies were sized up by Calvert Research and Management based on five factors—planet, workplace, customer, community, and shareholder. It used 230 performance indicators from Institutional Shareholder Services, MSCI, Sustainalytics, TruValue, Thomson Reuters Asset4, and Calvert's proprietary research.

The Rankings and Key Strengths

Any collection of dividend stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 10 publicly traded, most sustainable dogs are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here, April 23 data focused on 8 dividend payers.

Happily, 4 of those 8 dividend-paying Sustainable company stocks were found to be ‘Safer’ by showing free cash flow yields greater than their dividend yields. As of 4/23/26, the 4 are: Principal Financial (PFG), Analog Devices (ADI), Constellation Energy (CEG), and Lam Research (LRCX). None showed up as IDEAL, with dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price.

Many first-time investors regard the ideal of annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share prices as a buy signal or, at least, an invitation to look closer. The “Safer” ranking is given to stocks whose free cash flow yield exceeds their dividend yield. Paying dividends from cash flow is safer than using borrowed money.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 1.09% To 25.65% Net Gains From Ten Top ESG Companies To April 2027

All ten top 2026 ESG company stocks by yield were the top ten gainers for the current year based on analyst 1-year target prices. Thus, the yield-based forecast for these February ESG top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 100% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, supplied the data points. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable profit-generating trades projected to April 23, 2027 were:

Barron's Top Ten Sustainable February Dogs By Net Gains

Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG) was projected to net $266.45, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. A beta number was not available for CEG.

The Clorox Co. (CLX) was projected to net $223.46 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 19 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% less than the market as a whole.

Kimberly-Clark Corp. (KMB) was projected to net $215.57, based on the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 72% less than the market as a whole.

Lam Research Corp. (LRCX) was projected to net $166.67 based on the median of target estimates from 35 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 79% greater than the market as a whole.

CBRE Group (CBRE) was projected to net $132.15 based on the median of the target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% more than the market as a whole.

Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL) was projected to net $116.97 based on the median of target estimates from 12 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% over the market as a whole.

Owens Corning (OC) was projected to net $114.94 based on the median of target prices from 18 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% over the market as a whole.

Ormat Technologies (ORA) was projected to net $113.06 based on a median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 20% under the market as a whole.

Principal Financial (PFG) was projected to net $32.99, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 44% over the market as a whole.

Analog Devices (ADI) was projected to net $10.89 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 35 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 23.78% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends; (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

10 Barron's ESG Dogs Showed April Analyst Target Gains

10 Barron's ESG Dogs Showed April 1yr Total Return Gains

8 Barron's Sustainable Dogs Reveal April Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-18): 8 Top Barron's Sustainable Dogs By Yield For April

April 2026 Barron's Sustainable stocks represented six of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Two technology sector members placed first and fifth: Lam Research (LRCX) [1] and Analog Devices Inc. [5].

Then two utilities sector stocks placed second and third: Ormat Technologies [2] and Constellation Energy [3].

Two real estate representatives occupied the fourth and sixth slots: Jones Lang LaSalle [4] and CBRE Group [6].

Thereafter, one financial services member occupied the seventh place, Principal Financial [7].

Two consumer staples representatives nabbed the eighth and tenth places: Kimberly-Clark [8] and Clorox [10].

Finally, one industrials member took the ninth slot, Owens Corning [9], to complete the top ten April 2026 list of Barron's Sustainable ESG stocks by yield.

Barron’s Sustainable 10 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 4/23/26-7

The above chart reveals the eight dividend-paying Barron’s Sustainable Dogs subjected to this test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is displayed the cost per dividend-dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of these eight Sustainables is Kimberly-Clark at $19.13 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Lam Research at $248.63 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (19): Four of Eight Dividend-Paying Sustainable Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That means their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Four dividend-paying Sustainable stocks so infected in April are Ormat Technologies, Jones Lang LaSalle, and Kimberly-Clark. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, deals, tariffs, and artificial intelligence advice, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Eight Top-Yield Sustainable Stocks Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, none in the top ten are ideally priced. However, five of these ESG stocks are within $57 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split their shares. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (20-30) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 9.98% To 27.06% Top Eight Sustainable Upsides To April 2027; (32)Two -2.06% & -2.64% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 2.99% Disadvantage For The 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Sustainables As of April, 2027

Ten top Sustainables were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend/price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted above, the top ten Sustainables selected 4/23/26 by yield represented six of eleven sectors. Communications, Consumer Discretionary, Energy, Health Care, and Materials missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Sustainables (31) To Deliver 14.00% Vs. (32) 14.43% Net Gains by All Ten Come April 23, 2027

Analyst 1-year targets indicated $5,000 invested as $1,000 in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Sustainables delivered 2.99% LESS gain than $5,000 invested in all ten. The eighth lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, Constellation Energy, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 36.64%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield Sustainable dogs for April were: Principal; Clorox; Kimberly-Clark; Ormat; Owen Corning, with prices ranging from $97.00 to $125.66.

Five higher-priced Sustainable Top-Yield Dogs as of April 23 were: CBRE Group; Lam Research; Constellation; Jones Lang LaSalle; Analog, with prices ranging from $149.31 to $403.88.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As post-holiday froth in the market cools and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential deals gain momentum to flood 2026, with the exception of a few magnificent meme stocks, look for most of the top ten Sustainables to become Fair-priced… slowly but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Await 4 ‘Safer' April Sustainables To Become Fair-Priced

In mid-April 2026, 4 of the 10 Top Sustainables were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow yields greater than dividend yields. YCharts 4/23/26 data showed none as fair-priced.

Footnotes

Barron’s Sustainable Rankings as of 2026

Calvert Research and Management scored the 1,000 largest publicly traded companies across 230 environmental, social, and governance, or ESG, performance indicators. The above chart lists the 2026 10 top Sustainable dogs in order of their 2026 rankings. The right columns reveal the score and key sustainability strengths.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Sustainable Dog updates out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace). The 2026 Top 100 update is due out later this month from Barron’s.

The net-gain estimates mentioned above did not factor in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Sustainable dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation, or endorsement to buy or sell any security. data.title: AT&T Delivered A Double Beat, And The Lumen Integration Is Ahead Of Schedule
data.description:
AT&T (T) is setting the tone with the offense in 2026 as they delivered a Q1 revenue beat of $260 million after posting $31.5 billion, which was an increase of 2.8% YoY. There is a lot to like about AT&T after the recent pullback, as the dividend yield has increased to 4.24%. The Lumen integration is ahead of schedule, as the acquisition closed on February 2nd. This is critical for AT&T because it added 1.1 million fiber customers and over 4 million enabled fiber locations to its footprint. Some investors dislike the telecommunications sector because of the large capital investments required to maintain best-in-class services and the large debt loads that accumulate on the balance sheets. I look at AT&T as a new-age utility with an oversized dividend that is trading at a compelling valuation. Nobody is getting rid of their smartphones or internet connections as these become the backbone of communications. AT&T’s management just reiterated that they will generate $18 billion in free cash flow (FCF) during 2026, which is further evidence that they would still be extremely profitable after spinning out Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). I think that AT&T is undervalued at these levels, as they are adding subscribers and their forward earnings are expected to grow more than people expect.

Following up on my previous article about AT&T

The last time I wrote an article about AT&T was in May of 2025 (can be read here), and since then its share price has declined by -3.29%, while the S&P 500 has climbed 23.38%. The dividend has been the only thing keeping the total return from falling into the red. I had discussed that the rebound in its share price from the mid-teens was driven by strong FCF and refocusing its strategy on communication services. AT&T prioritized debt reduction, profitability improvements, and share buybacks, which should have positioned it for better results in the operating environment we experienced. I think that the market is wrong and AT&T is still a misunderstood company that is continuing to expand services and grow earnings for shareholders. I still believe there is an opportunity in AT&T, and the dividend yield is back over 4%, which is an added bonus. Furthermore, I’m long AT&T and believe that shares will be higher in the future, not lower.

AT&T delivered fantastic Q1 2026 results, and I am still long

I've been long on AT&T for years, and while there have certainly been easier places to make money, I’m staying long after reading the Q1 earnings report. I think that the market's 0.61 reaction was muted because GAAP EPS slipped to $0.54 from $0.61, but that headline misses the entire point. The Q1 2025 print included $1.4 billion of equity income from the DIRECTV stake, which is now gone. When I strip that point out and look at the adjusted EPS of $0.57, it was an increase of 11.8% YoY. AT&T’s adjusted operating income of $6.9 billion grew 7.8% YoY, and AT&T’s adjusted EBITDA increased 2.3% to $11.8 billion. When I see an operating margin expanding to 21.1% and service revenue growing 1.4% from AT&T, I get excited even though these numbers are not flashing extreme growth at discounted prices. What they do is all point in the same direction, which is what I want to see as an investor in AT&T because the days of deceleration seem to be behind us.

The bears will likely look at the FCF of $2.5 billion and complain that in Q1 2025, AT&T generated $3.1 billion. What they would be missing is that CapEx increased 15% YoY to $4.9 billion because management is intentionally accelerating fiber deployment. On the earnings call, Pascal Desroches (AT&T CFO) told analysts that AT&T expects to expand its fiber reach by roughly 8 million locations in 2026, which includes over 4 million from the Lumen acquired footprint. AT&T isn’t going to reach its goal of obtaining 60 million fiber locations by 2030 without spending more upfront. The full-year FCF guide of $18 billion or more is intact, and AT&T specifically cited that the Q1 FCF of $2.5 billion came in at the high end of its $2.0-$2.5 billion outlook.

When I went through the quarter, there were many operational wins that mattered, which are being overlooked in my opinion. This was the 6th quarter of more than 500,000 consumer and business net additions in advanced connectivity, which establishes a powerful forward trend. AT&T added 273,000 net AT&T Fiber customers and 239,000 Internet Air customers in Q1 in addition to the 1.1 million Lumen subscribers absorbed when the deal closed. The 584,000 total advanced internet net adds were a Q1 record, and the fiber footprint now sits at 37 million customer locations. This has reinforced AT&T’s path to 60 million-plus by 2030. Their Internet Air fixed wireless product runs on the same 5G infrastructure as the wireless business and continues to be the highest-margin growth lever in the portfolio because it utilizes existing spectrum and tower assets without a meaningful incremental build.

AT&T reported that 42% of advanced home internet households also subscribe to AT&T wireless, and I believe the market continues to miss this aspect. This number increases to 45% organically when you exclude the Lumen base, which carries a lower convergence rate. Lumen's existing fiber customers convert to AT&T wireless at less than 20%, and Lumen's overall fiber penetration sits around 25% versus AT&T's legacy fiber penetration of 40%. The delta between the two is AT&T’s upside, and if it closes across more than 4 million Lumen-acquired fiber locations, it should represent hundreds of thousands of incremental wireless additions and incremental fiber subscribers without any additional CapEx. This is the key because AT&T has already budgeted their slate, and they will be able to monetize their spend even further. AT&T also added 294,000 postpaid phone net additions, which was in line with expectations. The launch of AT&T OneConnect, which is the industry's first single-subscription product for fiber and wireless at a flat monthly price, is a direct attack against Verizon (VZ) and T-Mobile (TMUS), as they are still utilizing bundling to introduce savings.

Why AT&T is still undervalued and how the Fed setup in the back half of 2026 could be bullish

AT&T was a business that two years ago the market wouldn’t touch because of the WBD hangover and the lawsuit overhang from the lead-clad cable allegations. Both of those are behind AT&T. What is left is a domestic connectivity company with a fortress wireless business and a fiber business growing into a 60 million-location footprint. The valuation hasn't caught up to the fact that this is no longer the same company it was during the WBD era, and when I compare it to some other legacy businesses, I think telecom in general is getting the short end of the stick for no reason. I am comparing AT&T to Verizon (VZ) as a direct competitor to Costco (COST) and Walmart (WMT). The reasoning behind adding COST and WMT is because the two things people are not going without in 2026 or anytime in the future are food and connectivity. Both are necessities in 2026, and I think the market is missing that story.

AT&T is trading at $26.20 and is expected to generate $2.32 of EPS this year, $2.56 next year, and $2.89 the following year. AT&T trades at 11.3 times 2026 earnings and 9.1 times 2028 earnings, which represents 25.1% EPS growth over the next 2 years. VZ is trading at 8.1 times 2028 earnings, but their expected growth over this period is 16.8%, which is significantly lower than AT&T. When I look at WMT and COST, they trade between 45 and 50 times 2026 earnings and between 35 and 41 times 2028 earnings. WMT is expected to grow its EPS over the next 2 fiscal years by 27.5%, while COST has 21.6% of expected EPS on the horizon. When I consider that both are necessities and AT&T has a growth rate higher than COST and just under WMT, I get excited because this is a compelling value opportunity while getting paid a dividend that exceeds 4%.

AT&T is paying a dividend of $1.11, which is a 4.24% yield. The dividend has been stable since the WBD spinoff, and it's covered by FCF by a wide margin. AT&T is expecting to generate $18 billion in FCF during 2026, and the dividend is costing them roughly $8 billion. The payout ratio on FCF is roughly 44%, which leaves more than $10 billion for debt reduction, share repurchases (which AT&T has authorized $20 billion through 2027), and continued CapEx allocations. This isn’t a stressed dividend, as some felt it was back in the WBD era, as the yield is sustainable. AT&T shareholders are being paid a larger yield at this price point than what they could get from many risk-free assets, and I believe it makes the valuation case even stronger.

When I look at the debt analysis and how the Fed could act, I get more bullish for AT&T

The fed funds target sits at 3.50%-3.75%, and if we get rate cuts in the back half of the year, it will help expand AT&T’s margins. AT&T paid $1.81 billion in interest expenses in Q1, which was up 9.3% YoY. This is a $7.2 billion cash-interest run rate, and every 25 bps of relief on the refinancing curve translates to material dollars. The genuine savings opportunity sits in the high-coupon legacy paper, and most of it was issued during 2007-2010 when AT&T's funding costs were elevated. A meaningful portion of this paper is non-callable or has make-whole provisions that wouldn’t make sense to refinance at this time. After looking through the debt stack, AT&T has approximately $7-$8 billion of outstanding debt with coupons above 6.0%. A material portion of the debt is allocated towards 2030-2034 maturities. The weighted-average coupon on this high-cost stack is roughly 6.7%.

AT&T can refinance, call it $7.5 billion at today's BBB rates around 5.0%, and the annualized savings is approximately $128 million. If we get additional cuts, a post-cut environment could allow them to refinance at 4.5%, and the savings becomes $165 million. My bull case is that we could see 3 rate cuts over the next year, and if the 10-year drops meaningfully alongside Fed easing, AT&T could generate an annualized savings rate of $185 million. This isn’t a one quarter event, as it’s a structural reduction in fixed costs that runs until the end of each refinanced note. While some investors may not care about this savings, in my opinion every penny counts, and this will help expand margins and drive more profitability to the bottom line that shows up in the EPS each quarter.

The risks to investing in AT&T

While I am still bullish on AT&T, there are risk factors to consider. AT&T has underperformed the market, and there is no guarantee that AT&T will even match the market going forward. Investors have done significantly worse investing in AT&T over the years, and the dividend has not closed the gap. If CPI keeps printing in the low 3s and the Fed holds through year-end, the refinancing tailwind that I outlined becomes more unlikely. Management acknowledged that integration spending will be heavy this year due to the Lumen deal, and if they run into unexpected expenses, their margin forecasts could be impacted. TMUS and VZ have been more aggressive on pricing, and if they figure out a way to save customers more, AT&T could see its churn rates increase. Investors should do their own due diligence because even though I am bullish, I have been wrong on AT&T, and just investing in an index fund could generate larger returns.

Conclusion

AT&T just delivered its best Q1 ever for advanced internet net additions, they hit double-digit adjusted EPS growth, and they reaffirmed their $18 billion-plus FCF guidance. I think there is an opportunity in shares of AT&T, as the market isn’t giving their expected EPS growth from the end of 2026 through 2028 of 25.1% any respect. AT&T shares sold off recently, which caused the dividend to increase to 4.24%, which is a nice incentive for value investors. I think the bear case for AT&T requires a massive operational stumble, which I believe is improbable as it doesn’t reflect AT&T's underlying business metrics or management's execution. This is the type of setup that could do well in the future because the market doesn’t expect much from AT&T, and if they surprise to the upside, we could see shares rally quite a bit.

data.title: Summers Value Partners Q4 2025 Partner Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Partners:

The Summers Value Fund LP (“the Fund”) returned 6.7% net 1 in 2025, trailing the Russell 2000 Index ETF (IWM), which returned 12.7%, and the Russell 2000 Value Index ETF (IWN), which returned 12.4%. While our performance in 2025 fell short of expectations, the Fund’s long-term performance remains strong. Since inception, the Fund has delivered a 12.0% annualized net 1 return compared to 6.9% for IWM and 6.3% for IWN.

Trailing Period Returns Summers Russell 2000 Russell 2000
Value Fund LP 1 Small-Cap Index 2 Value Index 3
1 Year 6.7% 12.7% 12.4%
3 Years 57.8% 46.6% 38.6%
5 Years 90.3% 33.6% 51.3%
Cumulative Since Inception 4 136.4% 65.6% 59.3%
Annualized Since Inception 4 12.0% 6.9% 6.3%

Fund Commentary

In the first half of the year, the Fund generated a return of -12.8% net 1 . The healthcare sector faced significant pressure following the inauguration of the new administration in January. Sentiment weakened the following month with the nomination of a controversial Secretary of Health and Human Services. Tariff proposals targeting key trading partners added to broader economic uncertainty. Simultaneously, leadership changes and layoffs at the FDA created instability within one of the healthcare sector’s most important regulatory agencies. Investor concerns were further heightened by a government proposal aimed at curtailing drug prices in the United States. In addition, funding for early-stage biotechnology companies tightened sharply, forcing many companies to file for bankruptcy. Collectively, these factors led to a significant decline for healthcare stocks through June.

In sharp contrast, the Fund returned 22.4% net 1 in the second half of the year reflecting strong operating performance from our holdings and a more supportive backdrop for the healthcare sector. M&A activity accelerated in July, highlighted by several notable acquisitions, including Merck’s $10 billion purchase of Verona Pharmaceuticals and Sanofi’s $9.5 billion acquisition of Blueprint Medicines. Private equity interest also remained robust, with Archimed announcing the $750 million acquisition of Zimvie during the month. Additionally, second quarter earnings reports were broadly better than expectations. By this point, uncertainty surrounding federal government proposals and regulatory actions had been largely absorbed by market participants, contributing to improved investor confidence.

We believe the outlook for investing in the healthcare sector remains positive entering 2026 given low starting valuations, expectations for continued M&A and the potential for solid operating performance. We focus on identifying businesses that are more insulated from macro and regulatory factors and are positioned to compound value through a range of environments.

Top contributors in 2025 included Liquidia (LQDA), Ligand Pharmaceuticals (LGND) and Zimvie. Top detractors included Vestis (VSTS) and Indivior (INDV). The Fund held thirteen long positions and one short position at year-end. Our top three positions represented almost 45% of the portfolio, reflecting the concentrated nature of our strategy. Our top five holdings were Electromed (ELMD), Consensus Cloud Solutions (CCSI), Liquidia, ADMA Biologics (ADMA), and Ligand Pharmaceuticals. Our top five positions have all been held for one year or longer (almost eight years in the case of Electromed). We added new positions in Journey Medical (DERM), Avanos Medical (AVNS) and National Research Corp. We sold our longstanding position in Spok Holdings (SPOK). The Fund lost money on the short side of the portfolio in 2025 but remains profitable since inception.

The Fund's allocation to pharmaceutical and biotechnology stocks increased significantly during the year to over 40% by year end. We believe small-cap biotech and pharma stocks are on the front end of a multi-year innovation cycle. Recent drug launches from the likes of Liquidia, Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX), Kyrstal Bio, Ardelyx (ADRX) and Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) — among many others — have blockbuster revenue potential ($1 billion plus). In contrast, large-cap pharma companies are facing the largest patent expiry cycle in the industry's history. According to a recent article in The American Bazaar titled "The $400 Billion Patent Cliff: Big Pharma's Revenue Crisis," the industry could lose up to $400 billion of revenue by 2030. Mega blockbuster drugs such as Merck's (MRK) Keytruda, Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Eliquis, JNJ's (JNJ) Darzalex, Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Ozempic and Novartis' (NVS) Entresto could all lose key patent protections by the end of decade. Big pharma companies have strong balance sheets and generate substantial amounts of cash. In 2024, the top ten pharma companies globally generated over $120 billion of free cash flow. We believe acquisitions will play a key role in filling the void created by patent expirations, and small to mid-cap biotech and pharma companies are well-positioned to benefit as potential acquisition targets.

The Fund received $454,366 in dividend income during 2025. OTC Markets, our only non-healthcare holding, paid a special dividend of $1.75 in December. The Fund's dividend yield stands at 0.5%, reflecting our focus on long-term capital growth rather than income generation.

Largest Contributors

Liquidia (LQDA) – $3.6 billion market cap

We began accumulating a position in Liquidia last January during the JP Morgan Healthcare Conference. The company presented an initial cohort from the open label ASCENT trial of Yutrepia in patients with pulmonary hypertension - interstitial lung disease (PH-ILD), and the data looked exceptionally strong. At $1 billion, the company's market cap was overly discounted following years of litigation with its key competitor, United Therapeutics. Our physician research indicated patient dis-satisfaction with current therapies and a high unmet medical need in both pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and PH-ILD patients (both indications are in the Yutrepia label). At the time of our initial purchases, the company's lead asset, Yutrepia, had already received conditional approval by the FDA but awaited a key litigation outcome. That outcome came on May 2 allowing the FDA to give a final approval for the drug on May 23. Liquidia launched Yutrepia on June 2, and the initial launch phase has been nothing short of stunning with sales reaching $90 million in the fourth quarter. Yutrepia is poised to become a blockbuster in the quarters ahead due to its unique and differentiated profile, which includes easier titration and lower cough. Perhaps most remarkable, the company achieved positive cash flow of $30 million in the second full quarter post launch highlighting its efficient go-to-market model. Additional litigation remains outstanding with United, but we believe it will be concluded favorably for Liquidia in the coming months. If we are correct, the share price could have significant appreciation potential from here and the company could become an acquisition target. We are modeling earnings per share of $3.50 in 2026, which puts the current PE multiple at 12x.

Ligand Pharmaceuticals (LGND) – $3.9 billion market cap

We have owned Ligand for several years. As a royalty aggregator, Ligand has a capital light business model featuring attractive margins and high returns on capital. The company has a skilled management team that has a history of sourcing royalty rights on underappreciated drugs and medical devices with favorable risk-adjusted return profiles. The key to our thesis on Ligand has been the launches of Merck's (MRK) Ohtuvaryre for COPD and Travere's Filspari for kidney disease. Both drugs have blockbuster sales potential and long patent lives. In addition, we expect Palvella's (PVLA) QTORIN for MLM to be approved in 2027. QTORIN could also hold blockbuster potential with Ligand receiving a high single digit royalty on sales. We believe Ligand has a multi-year runway for better-than-expected sales and earnings growth as a result of these launches. Ligand's balance sheet remains strong with $1 billion of cash to deploy into future royalty deals.

Zimvie (ZIMV) – $730 million market cap

Zimvie was our largest detractor to performance in 2023-24. Our patience paid off in 2025 as the company was acquired by Archimed in July for a 124% premium. We sold our position with a marginal gain in September following the announcement.

Largest Detractors

Vestis (VSTS) – $890 million market cap

Vestis was spun off from Aramark in 2023 becoming a pure play workplace uniform rental business with exposure to healthcare. We were attracted to the stock given its low valuation and inferior margin profile relative to its two large publicly traded peers, Cintas and UniFirst. It was our belief that management could improve margins as a standalone company for many years to come. However, we underappreciated the amount of investment that was required to unlock higher margins. In addition, management turnover and balance sheet weakness created uncertainty, which contributed to a declining share price. We sold our position in May following a weaker-than-expected business outlook for 2025.

Indivior (INDV) – $4.2 billion market cap

We purchased shares of Indivior in October of 2024 at 4x EV/EBITDA and 1x EV/Sales – very cheap multiples for a high margin pharmaceutical company. The company had a valuable asset in Sublocade, a long-acting injectable therapy to treat substance abuse. Indivior had endured years of mismanagement and disappointment, which led up to the stock trading at an incredibly low valuation. In January, the company provided a worse-than-expected business outlook for 2025, which appeared to be the continuation of a long trend of missed expectations. We sold the stock as a result. As it turned out, our sale of Indivior represented our biggest mistake in 2025. The management team was replaced in February shortly after our sale. The company announced a new strategy that included cost cuts and a simplification of the business model. Following these positive changes, the stock increased by over 300%. We typically screen for new management changes like the one that occurred at Indivior, but we missed the opportunity to add it back to the portfolio.

Partnership Update

The firm achieved several meaningful milestones in 2025:

  • The Summers Value Fund LP reached a record amount of assets under management (AUM) of $40 million at year end. We launched the Fund with AUM of $2.6 million in 2018. We continue to believe our strategy has capacity for $500 million of AUM without compromising our opportunity set.
  • In January, we launched SVP Deal Fund III LP to increase our ownership stake in restor3d (private) through a convertible note offering. The convertible note was subsequently converted into a preferred equity security in July when private equity firm Partners Group led the Series B round. In total, we have invested almost $24 million in restor3d, which is targeting an IPO in late 2026 or early 2027.
  • We closed SVP Deal Fund 1 LP after returning $6 million of investor capital during the year. The Fund generated a return of 108% net of fees for an unlevered IRR of 21% net of fees over its life. We are proud of the return we were able to generate for our investors from this activist strategy, and we see the potential for additional activist opportunities in the future.

Our goal, as always, is to treat our investors the way we would want to be treated if we were on their side of the table. As in year's past, direct Fund expenses including fund administration, audit, tax and legal were covered through the operating budget. We have never charged a penny to the Fund beyond the stated management fee and incentive fee, when earned.

The Fund narrowly surpassed its 6% cumulative annual hurdle rate in 2025, which triggered a small incentive fee to the general partner.

As a reminder, the Summers Value Fund LP is — by far — my family's largest asset. I believe alignment between myself and our investors is a crucial driver of our long-term success.

In Closing

Thank you to our partners for your continued trust and support. Our stable and growing capital base is an important advantage in our goal to generate double-digit annual returns over the long run. We are grateful for those who added to their accounts during the year.

Our strategy continues to have ample capacity, and we welcome new investors who appreciate our unique approach. If you know someone who may be interested in learning more, please reach out to Alison Tomlinson at atomlinson@summersvalue.com.

Sincerely,

Andrew Summers, CFA, Managing Partner

Net returns are based on the management fee and incentive allocation applicable to Class B Interests (1.25% management fee; 20% incentive fee above a 6% annual cumulative hurdle rate).
