data.title: 5 Signs The Markets Are Increasingly Disconnected From Reality
data.description:
If a market pundit had appeared on Bloomberg on February 28th and stated these two things: 1) the Strait of Hormuz would remain effectively closed in late April and 2) the market would be at all-time highs despite all the negative impacts to the global economy due to only a trickle of traffic through this chokepoint, he would have met with a staggering amount of skepticism. Yet here we are.

As I noted in a recent article, there are few economic drivers in the U.S. economy that are not sputtering right now. The main one, outside massive fiscal deficit spending by the federal government, is the huge surge of tech spending on the AI infrastructure buildout. Quarterly results last week from Intel (INTC) and Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) showed again the power of the AI Revolution, at least for those firms that are supplying the 'picks and shovels' for this modern gold rush.

While I am happy my portfolio (roughly 30% short-term treasuries & cash/70% covered call holdings) is hugging all-time high-water marks, it is getting harder to believe the market reflects the reality of the current economic and geopolitical backdrop. Especially at these valuation levels. In today's article, I highlight five signs that the equity markets are increasingly disconnected from reality.

#1 - Consumer Sentiment

Consumer spending accounts for nearly 70% of the U.S. economy. A struggling consumer should be a significant headwind to both economic growth and stock market dynamics. And yet April's consumer sentiment readings hit their lowest level since data first started to be collected on this metric in 1978.

Think about that for a moment. Current U.S. consumer sentiment is lower than it was during the Stagflation of the late 1970s, below that of the double-dip recession of 1980-1982, and under that of the Internet Bust and 9/11. This reading is lower than the trough of the Great Financial Crisis or that of the Covid Pandemic.

There are many reasons for this consumer funk. Inflation has surged since the Covid lockdowns. Ironically, mostly originally due to global supply chain blockages similar to what is happening due to the Strait of Hormuz. Housing affordability remains historically low, job growth has been anemic since 2025, and consumer loan delinquencies are rising. Perhaps consumer sentiment has not impacted equity levels to this point due to the fact that the top 10% of American households by income own seven of every eight shares in the stock market. And 'The Haves' of the U.S. economy are doing quite well, especially after three years of outsized gains in the stock market from 2023 to 2025, following the big equity sell-off in 2022.

#2 - The Start Of AI-Related White-Collar Layoffs

And that dynamic might start to change if AI ushers in a White-Collar recession. AI-related layoffs have notably ticked up recently. Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) announced reductions in force last week that could impact up to 23,000 positions. This follows a recent disclosure by Oracle (ORCL) it will let go of up to 30,000 employees. These efforts are aimed at freeing up capital for these companies' massive AI infrastructure buildouts.

USPS, Amazon (AMZN), HSBC plc (HSBC), and Block (XYZ) are among notable companies recently announcing significant layoffs due to operational efficiencies brought about by improving AI capabilities. There is considerable debate on what portion of these layoffs are genuinely because of AI and how much is actually due to other factors such as the overhiring that followed Covid and tariff impacts.

However, if these AI-related layoff announcements accelerate and increasingly impact the white-collar workforce, consumer sentiment at the top end of the income strata could start to see measurable declines and impact consumer spending.

#3 - The Market Is On Auto Pilot

The S&P 500 closed at all-time highs as of the market close on Friday. Up a staggering $7.6 trillion since its recent bottom on March 30th. And this has happened despite no resolution to restoring traffic out of the Strait of Hormuz. A lot of this action seems like it is on autopilot. The NASDAQ recently had 13 straight winning trading sessions, the tech-heavy index's longest up streak since 1992. In another sign of irrational exuberance, the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) posted its 18th positive session in a row Friday and ended the week with an 11% gain.

I guess this makes some sense. A good chunk of daily volume is driven by momentum trading. In addition, other than companies making bombs, missiles, munitions, drones, and other defense goods, few sectors offer much earnings growth clarity right now.

#4 - Huge Surge Of Inflation Incoming

This conflict has triggered a huge surge in myriad energy and commodity prices. Gasoline prices in the U.S. have spiked to four bucks a gallon for the first time since 2022. Diesel has gone from $3.50 a share to $5.40 a share. This fuel is used extensively in the rail and trucking industries. This means the costs to transport goods to market have gone up substantially. Amazon, USPS, and other logistical providers have already added 'temporary' fuel surcharges for their customers.

Fertilizer prices have skyrocketed since this regional war began. This will start to show up as higher food prices with a lag in the quarters ahead. Therefore, it is hard to see how inflation levels do not head higher in coming months. This obviously will increase the chances that no further reductions are on the horizon for the Fed Funds rate. This surge in prices also increases the chances of Stagflation. Notably, gasoline prices surged just over 21% in March from February. The largest monthly rise ever recorded, and the key reason year-over-year CPI spiked to 3.3% in March, from 2.4% the previous month.

#5 - The Middle East Overhang

And here is the real disconnect of the market from economic reality. The last tankers that got through the Strait of Hormuz before this key transit point shut down in late February are now showing up in Asia, Europe, and even California. This is almost two months of normalized traffic lost through this passageway.

Even if a peace agreement were reached tomorrow and an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz signed, there are going to be ongoing and significant impacts on the global economy. Mines within the Strait will take many weeks or months to fully remove. Refineries, chemical plants, pipelines, and LNG facilities that have been damaged or destroyed throughout the Gulf region will take months or years to fully repair. The hundreds of millions of barrels of oil released throughout strategic petroleum reserves across the globe will take time to refill. These impacts will continue to affect the global economy throughout 2026 in a negative way.

And that is if a resolution is reached in short order. This feels increasingly unlikely. Each week this situation remains unresolved, the greater the negative impact on global supply chains and the world economy. And if the ceasefire doesn't hold and hostilities recommence, the negative impacts will cascade.

The $64,000 question is how much longer can the market remain this disconnected from economic and geopolitical reality? I guess we will find out together in the months ahead.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: Expansion Continues, Strong Q1 Results
data.description:
Today we pick back up on our extensive coverage of the regional bank Q1 earnings season. In today’s column we take a look at the key metrics out of one of the larger banks in our coverage universe, Huntington Bancshares (HBAN). While based out of Ohio, it really is not a regional bank but one that we could call a super-regional bank. It handles all sorts of banking but does have a primary focus on middle-market and commercial banking. The bank recently expanded significantly into the Sunbelt area of the U.S. as well. Over the years Huntington has been acquiring smaller players. Most recently it took on Texas-based Veritex a few months ago and also closed its $7.4 billion merger with Cadence Bank in February. These are some high-growth markets.

Looking ahead to 2026, we see growth on the horizon, and that includes the recent launch of its commercial banking segment in Austin, Texas. The bank’s Board recently also approved a massive $3 billion share repurchase authorization on top of reporting solid earnings. Let us discuss that Q1 performance.

Huntington Bancshares' Q1 2026 Earnings Headline Performance

In the headline results, the bank reported revenue of $2.59 billion, up 34% from last year, driven in part by some organic growth but also the aforementioned mergers we talked about above. It was a beat of $17 million against consensus. Q1 GAAP earnings per share of $0.25, which was weighed down by $263 million in acquisition-related expenses. However, the adjusted EPS of $0.37 surpassed consensus expectations, climbing from $0.34 a year ago, and was also in line with the sequential Q4. Again, these results and also what we are seeing with loans and deposits on the balance sheet follow the integration of Veritex and the closing of Cadence Bank. How about those loans and deposits?

Huntington Bancshares' Q1 2026 Loans and Deposits

We are always looking for growth in loans and deposits. Obviously with the mergers we see an artificial bump, but the bank has had no issue with growing these organically either. Average total deposits surged 18% sequentially to $204.6 billion and were up 27% from last year's Q1. Loan growth also saw big increases, with average total loans and leases increasing 19% from the sequential Q4 to $174.2 billion. While much of this is inorganic, the underlying organic growth remains there. We expect more growth organically from here as the bank expands its middle-market focus into high-growth areas like Austin, Texas. While some risk persists in commercial lending, particularly in office spaces with the rise of AI and remote work, commercial lending has held up based on our earnings reviews of many companies.

Margins Expand

Margins continue to be a bright spot for regional banks. So far, most, but not all, banks that we have covered have seen modest margin expansion. With the acquisition activity, it was unclear to us how margins would be impacted. Even though the cost of funds has been coming down, yields on loans have come down a bit in the last two years with rates being cut. In Q1, net interest margin expanded to 3.24%, up from 3.15% in the sequential Q4, and was up from 3.10% a year ago. This is a strong margin, and the 9 point sequential expansion was among the larger widenings we have seen.

Huntington Bancshares Asset Quality is Stable

Despite the rapid expansion from the acquisitions and some broader economic concerns with inflation and the Iran conflict, asset quality metrics were pretty solid in Q1. Net charge-offs came in at 0.26% of average total loans and leases. While this is a 2-basis-point softening from the sequential quarter’s 0.24%, it remains flat compared to a year ago and well below the average we have seen for peers we have examined. That is a strength.

And do not be fooled by the jump in the allowance for credit losses. These increased to $3.4 billion, but this was due to the Cadence acquisition. Instead, the better measure to focus on is the allowance for losses as a percentage of total loans. This was at 1.78%, flat from Q4. We did notice an uptick in nonperforming assets to 0.72%, which is something to watch as the Cadence integration continues toward its June system conversion.

Efficiency took a temporary hit due to merger costs, rising to 67.2%, but management is targeting a return to the 54% range by late 2026. Tangible book value per share stood at $9.55, up 9% from a year ago, despite the dilution of the all-stock deals we have talked about today.

Final Thoughts

With the recent moves into Texas, Huntington is one of the largest by deposits in Texas. The board’s new $3 billion share repurchase authorization signals massive confidence and helps to offset merger-related pressure on EPS. With Veritex now fully integrated, the full Cadence conversion should happen within the next two months. These moves are delivering growth opportunities across Texas and the South. We expect further growth for years to come for this super-regional bank. That said, at $16.40 a share, there is a sizable premium to the $9.55 tangible book. While finding any bank stock these days trading near tangible is hard to do, we would prefer to wait for a bit more pressure on the market and/or the sector for new money to establish a fresh position. But we like it as a quality hold for existing shareholders, with the growing dividend, acquisitions, and organic growth.

data.title: Las Vegas Sands: Q1 2026 Results - Trouble Ahead
data.description:
Summary

Las Vegas Sands (LVS) reported 1Q26 results, which caused the share price to fall 9%. The market focused on falling margins in Macau due to excessive market competition. I expect promotional spending by LVS to remain elevated, especially as competitors launch new offerings in Macau this year.

In addition, gaming growth in Macau is expected to slow as the year progresses due to a higher base effect in 2H25, so most of the upside to 2026 Macau gaming growth may already be behind us. Slowing gaming growth may lead to even more promotional activity, which will depress margins. I maintain my sell rating, with a 24% share price downside from current levels.

Premier gaming operator

Las Vegas Sands is one of the premier gaming operators in Asia with strong integrated resorts in Singapore and Macau. Key properties include Marina Bay Sands in Singapore and Venetian Macao. Despite the company's name, LVS does not have any gaming assets in the United States after LVS completed the sale of its Las Vegas resort assets in February 2022 to private equity group Apollo and VICI Properties.

1Q26 results were poor in Macau

LVS reported 1Q26 financial results on April 22. Adjusted property EBITDA grew by 24.6% YoY to $1.4bn, with EBITDA margins reaching 39.6% (-20 basis points YoY). By segment, LVS Singapore operations saw adjusted property EBITDA grow to $788m (+30.2% YoY), with EBITDA margin reaching 53% (+100 basis points YoY).

Macau, on the other hand, saw EBITDA increase to $633m (+18.3% YoY), with EBITDA margins declining to 29.9% (-140 basis points YoY). Other items of note were that LVS paid a $0.3 dividend per share during the quarter and repurchased $740m in outstanding shares.

Why did the share price fall 9% post earnings?

I published a Sell recommendation on April 15 for LVS, mainly on the concerns of rising competition within the premium player segment in Macau, which is the fastest-growing player segment. This scenario played out during 1Q26 as LVS management noted the environment remains 'intense' in terms of promotional activity.

Management also noted that it had favorable hold rates, and if the hold rates were normalized, EBITDA margin in Macau would have fallen by 200 basis points YoY instead of the reported -140 basis points. While Marina Bay Sands in Singapore continued to show excellent traction in terms of both growth and margins, LVS stock is still heavily driven by the Macao performance.

Competition is only going to grow

Management noted on its earnings call that Venetian Macao will introduce renovated suites in 3Q26 to compete within the premium mass segment. The new suites will enter into a competitive field that has released new product offerings in 2026.

Wynn opened a premium gaming area called Chairman's Club at Wynn Palace in February 2026. The Chairman's Club expanded by 3x in terms of area to now be at 100,000 square feet and features additional food/beverage amenities for the higher-end player. This new area resulted in strong gaming volumes and likely gaming market share gains for Wynn, as evidenced by a recent report from Citi.

MLCO will open its rebranded REM hotel in 2H26, formerly the Countdown Hotel, which has been closed since 2021 for renovation. REM will feature 150 suites with an average size of 1,000 square feet and have an avant-garde design. MGM Cotai will launch 124 premium suites in April. Galaxy Entertainment officially opened the luxury Capella Hotel in February, which features 95 penthouses and suites targeting the premium players.

LVS' Venetian integrated resort is not aimed at the premium player and is more for the middle-end spending customer base. Hence, LVS may not have the newer premium product suite to effectively compete against peers, which may result in even more discounting or promotional activity to attract players. I suspect not only LVS but all gaming operators are likely to continue to engage in competitive marketing to attract players, which could place further pressure on EBITDA margins and hurt profitability.

GGR is likely to slow

Total GGR (gross gaming revenue) for Macau grew by 9% in 2024 vs. 24% in 2023. YTD through March, GGR is +14%. At the beginning of April, analysts were expecting 10 to 12% YoY GGR growth for April. However, due to a lower casino hold rate in VIP, Citi recently cut its April GGR YoY growth forecast to +8 to 9%. Current consensus estimates for GGR growth in 2026 are around mid-single digits. GGR growth is expected to moderate in 2H26 due to a higher 2025 base effect.

Poor seasonality ahead and negative World Cup impact

The second quarter gaming environment in Macau is normally the weakest quarter of the year due to seasonality. Given a period where fewer players visit Macao. In addition, gaming revenues could be disrupted during major football (soccer) events.

Football is a popular sport in China, and players may wager heavily during major football events such as the World Cup and gamble less in casinos. The World Cup starts in mid-June 2026, which may cause weaker-than-expected casino revenues during the month-long tournament.

Further share price downside expected

LVS is currently trading at 8.6x forward EV/EBITDA, a 12% discount to the sector average and a 56% discount to its five-year mean. I would not use the five-year mean as a comparison base because this includes the COVID period, which saw disruption in gaming activity and the closure of casinos, which resulted in significant valuation volatility.

Given the many headwinds in terms of excess competition and slowing sector revenue growth, I expect additional downside for the shares and would wait to accumulate the shares around $40, which represents another 24% downside from current levels. $40 seems to be a technical bottom for the stock since 2012.

Upside risks

Upside risk, in my view, is if Macau monthly gaming revenues surprise on the upside, which typically results in a share price rally among all gaming stocks. Furthermore, if gaming operators become more rational in terms of promotions, adjusted property EBITDA margins could stabilize or grow. Also, if LVS announces an increase in dividends or share repurchase, this may also limit additional share price downside.

Conclusion

LVS is a premier gaming operator globally, but I am concerned with the margin downside, especially as LVS tries to compete in the competitive premium gaming segment in Macau. Without significant new capacity compared to peers, LVS may have to be even more promotional to attract players. This, coupled with slower GGR market growth in 2H26, makes me see another 24% downside for the stock and maintain my sell rating.
